rationale_for_baseline_tier: A new infected residual-limb ulcer with early surrounding cellulitis and a low-grade temperature, escalating over 4 hours in a compromised (amputated) limb, needs urgent same-day in-person assessment: the wound must be examined, swabbed and treated with antibiotics, and depth/osteomyelitis cannot be excluded remotely. It is more urgent than routine or prompt GP (T1/T2) because of the rapid escalation and compromised host, but there are currently NO systemic-sepsis features (no high fever, no confusion, no ascending erythema, no pus), so ED/000 (T4/T5) would be over-triage now. T3 (urgent same-day / urgent care) is correct, with a mandatory ED/000 safety-net if systemic features appear.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever >=38.5 C, rapidly spreading redness up the leg, purulent discharge, or new confusion
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of systemic sepsis from a spreading soft-tissue infection — a life-threatening emergency needing an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he spikes a fever of 38.5 or higher, the redness races up the leg, pus starts draining, or he becomes confused or drowsy — call 000 for an ambulance straight away and tell them it's a spreading wound infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain becomes completely unmanageable despite medication within about 12 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: An uncontrollable pain crisis needs urgent in-person analgesic management and re-evaluation for a missed deeper cause.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes completely unbearable and nothing helps over the next several hours, go to the emergency department or urgent care for stronger pain relief and a re-check.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness expands beyond the marked pen line, or the wound looks worse, by the next morning
escalation_edges.rationale: Spread beyond the marked border signals progressing cellulitis needing prompt same-day in-person reassessment and possible antibiotic change or admission.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Mark the edge of the redness tonight — if it has spread past your mark by morning, get seen the same day rather than waiting for the planned review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New calf swelling or pain, or sudden chest pain or breathlessness
escalation_edges.rationale: Reduced mobility raises VTE risk; these would suggest DVT/PE requiring emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your other calf swells or hurts, or you get sudden chest pain or shortness of breath, call 000 — that could be a clot.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A fall, especially with a head strike or new inability to move a limb
escalation_edges.rationale: Falls in an older amputee can cause fracture or head injury needing emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has a fall — particularly if he hits his head or can't move properly afterwards — go to the emergency department to be checked.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Amputated (compromised) limb with altered perfusion and reduced protective sensation
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for concern and for same-day review — wounds and infections in a residual limb progress faster and are harder to assess than in intact skin.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse present and assisting, but patient is wheelchair-bound with home trip hazards
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Enlist the spouse for stump inspection, redness-border marking and hazard removal, and confirm they can facilitate transport to the same-day assessment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult with new sedating analgesia and impaired mobility
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reinforce falls precautions; drowsiness from up-titrated gabapentin compounds an already-high falls risk.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange to be seen in person today — same-day GP, an urgent care centre, or hospital-in-the-home — so the wound can be examined, swabbed and treated with antibiotics and your pain relief sorted. Keep the prosthesis off, keep the leg elevated and rested, and mark the edge of the redness with a pen so any spread is easy to see.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day in-person GP or urgent care today; healthdirect 1800 022 222 for advice on the nearest option
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours home-visiting GP or the nearest urgent care / emergency department if it is worsening; do not wait for normal clinic hours given the rapid change tonight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring the current medication list, note the home temperature reading and the time the skin broke down, and show the marked redness border so the reviewer can judge any spread.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an infected compromised-limb wound as routine self-care or a routine 1-2 week GP appointment, or dismissing it as a phantom-pain flare.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progression to spreading cellulitis, abscess or osteomyelitis, sepsis, hospitalisation, or revision amputation.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Prompt-GP-in-24-to-48-hours instead of same-day assessment for a rapidly worsening compromised-limb infection.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending to ED or calling 000 when there are no systemic features — an urgent same-day in-person GP/urgent-care assessment is appropriate and less disruptive.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The depth of the infection and whether osteomyelitis is present cannot be excluded remotely, and the true extent of the erythema cannot be measured over chat.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Arrange urgent same-day in-person examination and swab, treat as early cellulitis of a compromised limb, and safety-net clearly for systemic features.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Diagnose and prescribe antibiotics over the phone, or reassure the patient that it is just a phantom-pain flare and defer to a routine appointment.
